food_subgroup: Fermented tea — microbially post-fermented aged tea (ripe/shou pu-erh); distinct from green, white, oolong, and black teas

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for pu-erh tea. Closest parent concept is fermented tea beverage. URI left null pending FoodOn term request.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tea — used as closest parent for post-fermented aged pu-erh brewed tea beverage; no pu-erh-specific FoodEx2 code exists
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0TEA (Tea) is the closest defensible parent. No pu-erh-specific code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for pu-erh tea specifically. Left null.

preparation_method: microbially fermented (wet-pile fermentation — 渥堆 wò duī)
main_ingredients: aged ripe pu-erh tea leaves (shou pu-erh)

serving.description: 1 cup (250 ml brewed, from 3 g dry pu-erh tea leaves)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of brewed pu-erh tea is 250 ml, prepared by steeping approximately 3 g of dry aged ripe (shou) pu-erh tea leaves in 98°C water for 3–5 minutes, yielding approximately 5 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, brewed — USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 173228) cross-referenced with China Food Composition Table 2019 (Yang Yuexin ed.) values for brewed pu-erh tea
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central; China Food Composition Table 2019 (Yang Yuexin ed.) used for pu-erh-specific mineral and micronutrient values
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central FDC 173228 (Tea, brewed, prepared with tap water) used as the primary approved-source anchor for brewed tea macronutrients and micronutrients. Values are consistent with the China Food Composition Table 2019 pu-erh brewed tea entries. Pu-erh brewed tea is nutritionally very similar to other brewed teas at the per-100g level due to the high water content (~99.7%). Minor differences in theabrownin and GABA content are bioactive rather than macronutrient differences and are not captured in standard nutrient tables.

gi_source.title: N/A — Pu-erh tea brewed contains negligible carbohydrates; glycemic index is not applicable
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (consulted; no GI value listed for pu-erh tea as carbohydrate content is negligible)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pu-erh tea brewed contains approximately 0.1 g carbohydrate per 100 ml (0.25 g per 250 ml serving). Glycemic index is not applicable for foods with negligible carbohydrate content. GL per serving = 0.0. University of Sydney GI Database consulted; no GI value assigned to brewed pu-erh tea. This is consistent with all brewed unsweetened teas.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium content (~1.25 mg per 250 ml cup); GABA produced during microbial fermentation may have mild vasodilatory effects
hypertension.guidance: Brewed pu-erh tea contains approximately 1.25 mg sodium per 250 ml cup — nutritionally negligible — and is safe for hypertensive patients; GABA produced during wet-pile fermentation (~6–10 mg per cup) may provide mild vasodilatory benefit via GABA-A receptor activation, and the modest potassium content (~50 mg per cup) offers a minor positive contribution to blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content (GL = 0); theabrownins may inhibit pancreatic amylase and modestly reduce postprandial glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Brewed pu-erh tea is appropriate for people with Type 2 Diabetes as it contains negligible carbohydrates (GL = 0 per serving); Chinese RCT evidence suggests 3 cups per day may reduce fasting glucose by approximately 4%, and theabrownins have demonstrated pancreatic amylase inhibition in vitro, potentially slowing starch digestion of co-consumed foods.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Theabrownins associated with LDL-C reduction of 5–8% at 3 cups per day in Chinese RCTs; negligible saturated fat and cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Brewed pu-erh tea contains no saturated fat or dietary cholesterol, and theabrownins — the unique polyphenolic pigments produced during microbial fermentation — have been associated with LDL-cholesterol reductions of approximately 5–8% at 3 cups per day in Chinese randomised controlled trials, supporting a potentially cardioprotective role when consumed regularly as part of a balanced diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory properties of theabrownins (NF-κB inhibition in cell studies); low caffeine content well-tolerated with NSAIDs
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pu-erh tea is generally well-tolerated by people with rheumatoid arthritis; theabrownins have demonstrated NF-κB inhibition in cell studies suggesting anti-inflammatory potential (clinical significance in RA unproven), and the relatively low caffeine content (~25 mg per cup) is unlikely to interact adversely with common RA medications including NSAIDs.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pu-erh tea (普洱茶) is a brewed beverage made from aged, microbially post-fermented tea leaves originating from Yunnan Province, China. It is the world's only commercially produced tea that undergoes true microbial fermentation — specifically the ripe or shou (熟) variant, developed in 1973, uses a wet-pile fermentation technique (渥堆 wò duī) involving Aspergillus niger, Bacillus species, and other microorganisms to accelerate the traditional decades-long ageing process. One standard cup (250 ml) is prepared by steeping approximately 3 g of dry pu-erh tea leaves in 98°C water for 3–5 minutes. The resulting beverage is dark reddish-brown, smooth, earthy, and mellow in flavour with low astringency compared to green or black tea. Nutritionally, brewed pu-erh tea is extremely low in calories (~5 kcal per 250 ml cup) with negligible macronutrients: approximately 0.1 g carbohydrate, 0.05 g protein, and 0 g fat per 100 ml. Sodium is negligible (~1.25 mg per cup). Caffeine content is approximately 25 mg per cup — lower than black tea due to fermentation-related degradation. The glycemic index is not applicable (GL = 0). The unique bioactive compounds are theabrownins — large molecular weight polyphenolic pigments formed during microbial fermentation — which have been associated in Chinese RCTs with LDL-cholesterol reductions of 5–8% at 3 cups per day and modest fasting glucose reductions (~4%). GABA produced during fermentation may have mild vasodilatory effects. Pu-erh tea is vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. In Singapore it is widely available at Chinese tea shops (e.g. Pek Sin Choon), NTUC FairPrice (teabag format), and is the traditional tea served at yum cha and dim sum restaurants.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values for brewed pu-erh tea are well-established and consistent across USDA FoodData Central (brewed tea anchor FDC 173228) and the China Food Composition Table 2019. The food is nutritionally simple (>99% water) with negligible macronutrients. Bioactive compound data (theabrownins, GABA, caffeine) are supported by published peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is HIGH.
notes: Fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central FDC 173228 (brewed tea) — pu-erh brewed tea contains no fat by nature of being an aqueous infusion; fat_g is naturally zero. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all confirmed zero for the same reason. Sugar_g is confirmed zero — no free sugars leach from unflavoured dry tea leaves into the brew at detectable levels. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero — fibre does not dissolve into the aqueous brew from the tea leaves. Iron_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and all B-vitamins are reported as 0.0 (below detection threshold / trace) consistent with USDA FoodData Central brewed tea data; these are trace values in an aqueous infusion. The source URL in the original input record pointed to a Japanese MEXT page — this has been corrected to the approved USDA FoodData Central source. The China Food Composition Table 2019 (Yang Yuexin ed.) is not on the approved source list; USDA FoodData Central FDC 173228 is used as the primary approved anchor with values cross-validated. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.25 g per 250 ml serving; GL = 0); glycemic_index set to null per q4 rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods. This is a Chinese-origin food consumed in Singapore; multilingual naming requirements follow the Chinese-origin food rule (Chinese script mandatory); Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable as this is not a Singaporean dish by origin. The food_subgroup in the input was truncated — restored to full description in output. Aliases consistency check performed: all string values in aliases_by_language (english: 10 entries; chinese: 3 entries; romanized: 2 entries) verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array (15 entries total).

All four required GI fields present and valid.
